article_meta.title: Iranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article objectively and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words, neutral tone)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items — the 5 most objectively significant facts
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective or source type included
- Do not editorialize, adopt the article's framing, or use loaded language
- Represent all perspectives present in the article proportionally

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: Iranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026
SOURCE: The Guardian

ARTICLE:

Iranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026. At least 6,000 deaths have been verified by one human rights organisation, which is investigating more that 17,000 more. Photograph: MAHSA/Middle East Images/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenIranians block a street during a protest in Tehran, Iran on 9 January 2026. At least 6,000 deaths have been verified by one human rights organisation, which is investigating more that 17,000 more. Photograph: MAHSA/Middle East Images/AFP/Getty ImagesRights and freedomIranIran accused of âcampaign of revengeâ as doctors arrested for treating protesters US state department calls for the release of all detained healthcare workers as at least one arrested surgeon reported to be at risk of executionSupported byAbout this contentDeepa ParentThu 29 Jan 2026 00.00 ESTLast modified on Thu 29 Jan 2026 14.21 ESTShareDoctors are being arrested in Iran for helping save the lives of some of the tens of thousands injured during Iranâs brutal crackdown on anti-regime protests, with at least one surgeon now at risk of being sentenced to death.The arrests and death sentence are part of a campaign of ârevengeâ, say human rights groups, after healthcare workers and doctors refused to ignore the plight of badly injured protesters shot or stabbed at close range, and in some cases set up makeshift treatment centres.An Iranian surgeon, Alireza Golchini, 52, from the central city of Qazvin, has been charged with âmoharebehâ (waging war against God), which can carry the death penalty, according to the Norway-based rights group Hengaw. The US state department yesterday called for his release.The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency, whose figures have been reliable during previous crackdowns, says it has verified more than 6,000 dead and has more than 17,000 more recorded deaths under investigation.0:46Bodies line the streets outside a morgue in Tehran as protests continue â videoSpeaking to the Guardian, his cousin, Nima Golchini, who is based in Canada, said that Golchini was taken from his home on 10 January. âHe was arrested in a violent manner in front of his wife and son, who is only 11. They beat him up so badly during arrest, they broke his arm, ribs and dragged him out of his home. My family is terrified.âA few days before his arrest Golchini, who also treated protesters during the 2022 Woman, life, freedom protests, had posted a note on his social media, says Nima, sharing his number and asking injured patients to contact him for treatment.âAll he did was his duty of saving lives as a medical doctor. He had sworn to save peopleâs lives. How can any doctor not stand by his oath? I am worried not only for him, but also for other healthcare workers who have been arrested for simply standing by their sworn oath.âIranian authorities have not publicly commented on Golchiniâs detention, nor have they confirmed any charges against him. But Iranâs judiciary chief, Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei, has urged authorities not to show any leniency towards protesters. âWe should not remain silent in the face of those who seek to exploit the situation and disrupt the security and calm of the people,â he said.View image in fullscreenThe Iranian surgeon Alireza Golchini, who studied in London, was arrested and reportedly charged with âwaging war against God Photograph: suppliedGolchini is one of at least nine doctors and healthcare volunteers arrested over the past week, say rights groups and medics. According to the Norway-based Iran Human Rights (IHRNGO), security forces raided makeshift medical shelters as well as the homes of doctors and volunteers who treated injured protesters. It said there is no information now on the whereabouts or condition of those detained.Iran protest doctor: âIn one street, I saw blood pooled in a gutter with a trail stretching several metresâ | AnonymousRead moreâThis appears to be a deliberate revenge campaign against doctors and medical staff who refuse to abandon the wounded,â said Hossein Raeesi, an Iranian human rights lawyer living in exile.IHRNGO also reported the arrest of a volunteer first responder who had turned his home into an improvised medical shelter. According to the source, he was detained on 14 January after security forces raided his house, where he had provided care to more than 20 injured protesters, two of whom later died.âHe was taken away in an extremely brutal manner and was severely beaten,â a source told IHRNGO, adding that security forces smashed the windows of the house, destroyed the interior and severely damaged his car during the raid.At least 42,324 arrests have been made across the country with limited information on their fate, according to the US-based Human Rights Activists in Iran, which says the regime is putting pressure on medical networks as a means of reducing support for the injured.âThis persecution of medical personnel is yet another dimension of the regimeâs crimes against humanity,â said Mahmood Amiry-Moghaddam, IHRNGO director.A statement published yesterday by the US state department on X demanded the release of Golchini and âall the brave doctors who have helped their fellow countrymenâ. It continued: âPresident Trump has clearly stated that no executions should take place in Iran and that there will be consequences if the government takes such actions.âExplore more on these topicsIranRights and freedomMiddle East and north AfricaProtestnewsShareReuse this contentMost viewedMost viewed
---

{
  "summary": "Iranian authorities are conducting a campaign of arrests targeting doctors and healthcare workers who treated protesters injured during the government's crackdown on anti-regime demonstrations. At least nine medical professionals have been detained in the past week, including surgeon Alireza Golchini, 52, who faces charges of \"waging war against God,\" which carries the death penalty. Golchini was violently arrested on January 10, 2026, suffering broken bones during the detention. Security forces have raided makeshift medical shelters and homes of volunteers providing care to injured protesters. Human rights organizations report that at least 6,000 deaths have been verified, with over 17,000 more under investigation, and more than 42,000 arrests have been made nationwide. The US State Department has called for the release of detained healthcare workers, with President Trump warning against executions. Human rights groups characterize these actions as a deliberate revenge campaign against medical personnel who refused to abandon wounded protesters.",

parsed_output.summary: Iranian authorities are conducting a campaign of arrests targeting doctors and healthcare workers who treated protesters injured during the government's crackdown on anti-regime demonstrations. At least nine medical professionals have been detained in the past week, including surgeon Alireza Golchini, 52, who faces charges of "waging war against God," which carries the death penalty. Golchini was violently arrested on January 10, 2026, suffering broken bones during the detention. Security forces have raided makeshift medical shelters and homes of volunteers providing care to injured protesters. Human rights organizations report that at least 6,000 deaths have been verified, with over 17,000 more under investigation, and more than 42,000 arrests have been made nationwide. The US State Department has called for the release of detained healthcare workers, with President Trump warning against executions. Human rights groups characterize these actions as a deliberate revenge campaign against medical personnel who refused to abandon wounded protesters.
key_facts: At least nine doctors and healthcare workers arrested in Iran for treating injured protesters, with surgeon Alireza Golchini facing potential death penalty charges
Human rights organizations have verified more than 6,000 deaths and are investigating over 17,000 additional recorded deaths from the crackdown
Over 42,000 arrests have been made across Iran with limited information about detainees' fates
Security forces raided makeshift medical shelters and homes of doctors, with reports of violent arrests causing injuries to detainees
US State Department demanded release of detained medical personnel, with President Trump warning of consequences if executions proceed
sources_represented: Human rights organizations (Hengaw, IHRNGO, Human Rights Activists News Agency)
Family member of detained surgeon (Nima Golchini)
